title: Source lineage for CME00107: Life of Saint Brendan
summary: CME00107 is the repository copy of the University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse encoding of Carl Horstmann's 1874 edition of the Middle English Life of Saint Brendan. Horstmann printed an Ashmole 43 base text and supplied variant apparatus from Harley 2277, Laud Misc. 108, and Bodley 779. Complete Digital Bodleian image sequences are locally cached for the three Oxford witnesses and mapped to the exact work folios; the British Library currently exposes a catalog record and legacy viewer link for Harley 2277, but its images were unavailable when checked. Horstmann's route to the Harley readings is modeled through Thomas Wright's 1844 printed transcription because that is the intermediary Horstmann explicitly cites; direct manuscript consultation remains unresolved.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00107
notes: The file is 160,901 bytes and is pinned through the recorded CME submodule commit.
Its 733 main-text L elements are followed by serialized variant apparatus; the printed edition physically interleaves Harley variants below the Ashmole text on pp. 17-37.

entities.label: University of Michigan CME encoding CME00107
entities.description: A manually keyed, basic-TEI electronic text of Horstmann's complete 1874 installment, including the Ashmole base text and the Harley, Laud, and Bodley apparatus.
notes: The item advertises LANG='enm ger'; the Middle English work and German editorial apparatus are distinct layers.
The exact item URL was listed publicly but was protected by an automated-access challenge when checked.

entities.label: Carl Horstmann's 1874 edition of the Life of Saint Brendan

bibliographic.citation: Carl Horstmann, 'Die altenglische Legende von St. Brendan aus Ms. Ashmol. 43, fol. 71b,' Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Literaturen 53 (1874): 17-48.
bibliographic.title: Die altenglische Legende von St. Brendan aus Ms. Ashmol. 43, fol. 71b
bibliographic.container_title: Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Literaturen

notes: The poem and embedded Harley variants occupy pp. 17-37; the remaining Laud and Bodley apparatus extends through p. 48.
The article closes '(Fortsetzung folgt.)'. The encoded installment is complete, but whether the promised continuation appeared elsewhere remains unresolved.

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of Archiv volume 53 (1874)
entities.reproduction_format: Complete 492-page PDF scan with whole-volume OCR and Internet Archive metadata

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS Ashmole 43
entities.description: The principal early-fourteenth-century South English Legendary witness and Horstmann's base text for CME00107; the Life of Saint Brendan occupies ff. 71v-80v.

notes: Written by one small Anglicana hand.
The catalog localizes the manuscript's orthography to Gloucestershire, eLALME LP 7170; that is witness evidence, not a precise composition place for the poem.

entities.label: Digital Bodleian complete IIIF sequence for MS Ashmole 43
entities.reproduction_format: 580-canvas IIIF sequence; cached as a ZIP containing every width-1600 JPEG derivative, the provider manifest, and an inventory
notes: The Life of Saint Brendan maps exactly to canvases 156-174, ff. 71v-80v.

entities.label: British Library Harley MS 2277
entities.description: A South English Legendary manuscript of circa 1300. The Life of Saint Brendan occupies ff. 41v-51r and supplied Wright's 1844 metrical text, which Horstmann cited for his Harley variants.

notes: Produced in South-West England and written in Gothic cursive according to the current catalog.
The target's Somerset linguistic localization is witness evidence, not a poem-composition place.

entities.label: Thomas Wright's 1844 metrical Life of Saint Brendan

bibliographic.citation: Thomas Wright, St. Brandan: A Medieval Legend of the Sea, in English Verse and Prose (London: Percy Society, 1844), metrical text pp. 1-34.
bibliographic.title: St. Brandan: A Medieval Legend of the Sea, in English Verse and Prose

notes: Only Wright's metrical text from Harley 2277 is in scope here; his separately printed prose life from de Worde's Golden Legend is not a source for CME00107.
Wright states that his metrical text was printed from Harley MS 2277, f. 41v.

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of Wright's St. Brandan (1844)
entities.reproduction_format: Complete Google Books-derived PDF with whole-volume OCR and Internet Archive metadata

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS Laud Misc. 108
entities.description: A late-thirteenth- or early-fourteenth-century South English Legendary witness used in Horstmann's variant apparatus. The surviving Life of Saint Brendan occupies ff. 104r-110r and lacks lines 68-247 because two folios were lost after f. 104.

date_statements.label: target witness date
date_statements.value: late thirteenth or early fourteenth century

notes: The catalog's broader 1290-1400 range includes later additions; the Brendan text belongs to the principal early core and hand A.
The manuscript is English, possibly associated with Oxford; this is not a proven composition place for the poem.

entities.label: Digital Bodleian complete IIIF sequence for MS Laud Misc. 108
entities.reproduction_format: 496-canvas IIIF sequence; cached as a ZIP containing every provider-native image, the provider manifest, and an inventory
notes: The surviving Life of Saint Brendan maps exactly to canvases 219-231, ff. 104r-110r.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS Bodley 779
entities.description: A second-quarter-fifteenth-century South English Legendary witness used in Horstmann's variant apparatus. Its Life of Saint Brendan occupies ff. 1r-1v and 3r-10r, interrupted by a contents leaf at f. 2.

notes: Written by one scribe in multiple Anglicana phases.
The catalog associates the orthography with Hampshire, eLALME LP 5470; this is witness evidence, not the work's composition place.

entities.label: Digital Bodleian complete IIIF sequence for MS Bodley 779
entities.reproduction_format: 672-canvas IIIF sequence; cached as a ZIP containing every width-1600 JPEG derivative, the provider manifest, and an inventory
notes: The work maps to canvases 9-10 and 13-27; canvases 11-12 reproduce intervening f. 2 and are outside the poem.

entities.label: DIMEV record 4567 for the Life of Saint Brendan
notes: DIMEV identifies seventeen witnesses and reports 738 lines in couplets. This census is broader than Horstmann's four-witness edition and is not modeled as seventeen direct source relations.
The record's 738-line extent differs from the 733 main-text L elements in CME00107 and from other scholarly counts.

rights.component: Underlying anonymous medieval Life of Saint Brendan
rights.basis: The work was composed in the thirteenth century and its author is anonymous; any copyright term has expired.
The edition was published in 1874; Internet Archive records the volume as not in copyright in the United States.

rights.component: U-M electronic transcription and markup for CME00107

notes: This records the collection-level statement; it is not a representation that all interface assets are freely licensed worldwide.

rights.basis: The file is a repository copy of the U-M CME public-domain-source encoding; the repository's code license does not by itself license every data component.
The edition was published in 1844 and Internet Archive records the item as not in copyright.

editorial_practices.source_feature: The Life of Saint Brendan within the larger South English Legendary tradition and within four codices
editorial_practices.representation: An Ashmole 43 running text with three witness-specific variant apparatuses
editorial_practices.description: Horstmann edited one work from a fluid multi-witness compilation rather than reproducing an entire manuscript or all known witnesses.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Ashmole 43 base text and readings associated with Harley 2277, Laud Misc. 108, and Bodley 779
editorial_practices.representation: Running base transcription, embedded Harley notes, and separate Laud and Bodley variant lists
editorial_practices.description: The edition distinguishes a base witness from comparison witnesses; it is not a diplomatic facsimile or a witness-neutral reconstruction.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Abbreviated or supplied manuscript letter sequences
editorial_practices.representation: Typographically differentiated spans, commonly italicized and encoded as HI in CME00107
editorial_practices.description: Direct comparison with Ashmole images supports using the differentiated type as evidence of editorial intervention, but Horstmann's article supplies no inspected global key proving that every italic span has one identical function.

editorial_practices.source_feature: The complete article, with Harley readings physically interleaved beneath the running poem
editorial_practices.representation: The main poem is serialized first, followed by a Harley variant DIV2 and then Laud and Bodley DIV2 elements
editorial_practices.description: XML reading order does not preserve the page-level interleaving of base text and Harley variants, although it preserves the variant content and printed page milestones.

evidence.citation: General Corpus, CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00107.xml, at CME submodule commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for CME identifiers, title and editor, UMDL handle, language declaration, printed-page milestones, the Ashmole base-text heading, three variant headings, encoded structure, and the closing continuation notice.

evidence.citation: Programmatic structural audit of the pinned CME00107 XML.
evidence.locator: DIV1, DIV2, HEAD, LG, L, NOTE, PB, MILESTONE, P, and HI boundaries
evidence.summary: The XML contains one DIV1, three DIV2 variant divisions, four HEAD elements, two LG elements, 737 total L elements, 733 main-text L elements, four Harley-apparatus L elements, 156 NOTE elements, 53 PB elements, 164 MILESTONE elements, 28 P elements, and 1,081 HI elements.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse collection description and CME title list.
evidence.summary: Describes a public corpus manually keyed from public-domain editions, in-house proofread, encoded in basic TEI, and offered as bulk XML; the title list binds CME00107 to Horstmann's edition. The collection applies a No Copyright - United States statement to its materials.

evidence.citation: Carl Horstmann, 'Die altenglische Legende von St. Brendan aus Ms. Ashmol. 43, fol. 71b,' Archiv 53 (1874): 17-48.
evidence.summary: Primary edition evidence for Horstmann's title, publication, Ashmole base-text claim, Harley/Wright statement, Laud and Bodley variant headings, page boundaries, editorial typography, and continuation notice.

evidence.citation: Visual inspection of the complete Horstmann article and its position in Archiv volume 53.
evidence.summary: Confirmed that the complete article begins on printed p. 17, interleaves Harley variants beneath the running Ashmole text through p. 37, continues with Laud and Bodley apparatus, and closes on p. 48 with '(Fortsetzung folgt.)'.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata for archivfrdasstu53brauuoft.
evidence.summary: Identifies the complete volume and derivative files and records NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT status in the United States. Its generic date field says 1846, but the inspected title page establishes this volume as 1874.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, MS. Ashmole 43.
evidence.summary: Supplies the work boundary, incipit and explicit, date, support, extent, dimensions, hand, English origin, and Gloucestershire orthographic localization.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian IIIF manifest for MS. Ashmole 43.
evidence.summary: Supplies a complete manuscript sequence, exact canvas labels and image services, provider attribution, CC BY-NC 4.0 terms, and the cached whole-object inventory.

evidence.citation: Visual inspection of MS. Ashmole 43 ff. 71v and 80v in the cached Digital Bodleian sequence.
evidence.summary: Confirmed the rubric and decorated opening on f. 71v and the poem's ending on f. 80v immediately before the following Life of Saint Samson.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts, Harley MS 2277, record 040-002048108.
evidence.locator: South English Legendary, ff. 1r-232v; target boundary additionally established by PCMEP and DIMEV
evidence.summary: Supplies the current shelfmark, codex date and origin, support, dimensions, script, language, holding institution, physical-access guidance, and legacy digitization link. The linked images were unavailable when checked.

evidence.citation: Thomas Wright, St. Brandan: A Medieval Legend of the Sea, in English Verse and Prose (London, 1844).
evidence.locator: title page, preface pp. v-viii, metrical text pp. 1-34
evidence.summary: Supplies publication data and the complete printed metrical text from Harley MS 2277 used as Horstmann's explicitly cited prior edition.

evidence.citation: Thomas Wright, preface to St. Brandan (1844), pp. v-viii.
evidence.summary: Wright states that the metrical legend was printed from Harley MS 2277, beginning at f. 41v. His prose selection is a distinct work and is outside CME00107's lineage.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata for stbrandanamedie01musegoog.
evidence.summary: Identifies the complete scan derivatives and records NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT status.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, MS. Laud Misc. 108.
evidence.summary: Supplies the work boundary, rubric, incipit and explicit, missing-leaf lacuna, codex date and stratification, support, extent, dimensions, hand, and broad origin.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian IIIF manifest for MS. Laud Misc. 108.
evidence.summary: Supplies a complete manuscript sequence, exact canvas labels and image services, provider attribution, CC BY-NC 4.0 terms, and the cached whole-object inventory.

evidence.citation: Visual inspection of MS. Laud Misc. 108 ff. 104r and 110r in the cached Digital Bodleian sequence.
evidence.summary: Confirmed the Latin rubric and decorated Brendan opening after the preceding Saint Patrick text on f. 104r and the poem's final lines and Amen on f. 110r.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, MS. Bodley 779.
evidence.locator: Life of St Brendan, ff. 1r-1v and 3r-10r
evidence.summary: Supplies the discontiguous work boundary, false Saint Patrick rubric and corrected Brendan incipit, date, support, extent, dimensions, hand, English origin, and Hampshire orthographic localization.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian IIIF manifest for MS. Bodley 779.
evidence.summary: Supplies a complete manuscript sequence, exact canvas labels and image services, provider attribution, CC BY-NC 4.0 terms, and the cached whole-object inventory.

evidence.citation: Visual inspection of MS. Bodley 779 ff. 1r, 2r, 3r, and 10r in the cached Digital Bodleian sequence.
evidence.summary: Confirmed Brendan's opening under a false Saint Patrick rubric on f. 1r, a contents list outside the poem on f. 2r, resumption on f. 3r, and the final Amen before a new Saint Patrick text on f. 10r.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian, Terms of Use.
evidence.summary: Explains the CC BY-NC framework for noncommercial reuse and the need to seek permission for commercial uses; the exact manuscript manifests provide the item attribution and license.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, record 4567, IMEV/NIMEV 2868, version 1.0 data release.
evidence.locator: record 4567 and seventeen linked witness entries
evidence.summary: Identifies the work by incipit, title, subjects, aa couplet form, a 738-line extent, and seventeen witnesses including Horstmann's four sources; the release is CC BY 4.0.

evidence.citation: Production and Use of English Manuscripts 1060 to 1220? / PCMEP, text details for Saint Brendan.
evidence.summary: Provides the poem's 1250-1290 composition range with circa 1260 suggested, broad southern dialect assessment, septenary aa-couplet form, DIMEV/IMEV identifiers, Harley boundary ff. 41v-51r, and a 733-line count described as 732 lines plus final Amen.

evidence.citation: Simon Lavery, 'The Source of the St Brendan Story in the South English Legendary,' Leeds Studies in English, n.s. 15 (1984): 21-32.
evidence.summary: Argues that the Latin Navigatio Sancti Brendani is probably the poem's principal source, rejects the Anglo-Norman Voyage of Benedeit as a secondary source, characterizes the poem as an abridged vernacular didactic/homiletic saints' life, and reports a 734-line extent.

open_questions.question: Did the continuation promised at the end of Horstmann's 1874 installment appear in a later issue or another publication, and what material was intended to continue?
next_steps: Search later Archiv volumes and Horstmann bibliographies for the article title and opening phrase.
Do not call the underlying edition project complete or incomplete solely from the parenthetical notice; call pp. 17-48 the complete encoded installment.

open_questions.question: Did Horstmann collate Harley MS 2277 directly or derive the Harley apparatus entirely from Wright's 1844 edition, and will the British Library restore the known digital image sequence?
next_steps: Consult Horstmann's working papers, correspondence, or a fuller methodological statement.
Compare the Harley apparatus against Wright and the manuscript when the legacy images return or new reproductions are supplied.
Retain Wright as the supported intermediary until direct consultation is evidenced.

open_questions.question: How should the competing extents of 733 main XML lines, DIMEV's 738 lines, Lavery's 734 lines, and PCMEP's 732 lines plus final Amen be reconciled across witnesses and editorial counting conventions?
next_steps: Collate lineation in Horstmann, Wright, the four manuscripts, and modern editions.
Record whether rubrics, Amen, split lines, omissions, and witness-specific additions are counted.
Keep the XML structural count separate from abstract-work and witness counts.

open_questions.question: Which Latin Navigatio Sancti Brendani textual branch or physical exemplar most directly underlies the South English Legendary poem?
next_steps: Use Lavery's study as evidence for probable work-level dependence, not for an identified physical source.
Consult subsequent textual scholarship and compare distinctive readings against Latin Navigatio recensions.
Keep the rejected direct dependence on Benedeit's Anglo-Norman Voyage separate from the broader shared Brendan tradition.

review_notes: All three Oxford facsimile caches contain the complete provider-exposed manuscript sequence, not only the leaves containing the work; exact work portions are recorded with canvas and folio boundaries.
The seventeen-witness DIMEV census is retained as catalog evidence rather than flattened into Horstmann's direct lineage.
Manuscript date, production place, and dialect are kept distinct from work composition, and scan-file rights are kept distinct from public-domain textual content.
